planting.note: Plant corm divisions (pups/suckers) in spring after last frost in full sun in rich, deeply cultivated, well-drained, moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil; in zone 9, plantains fruit reliably as perennials and should be mulched heavily in winter to protect corms from the occasional hard freeze.

directions: Plant suckers in deeply cultivated, rich, well-drained, moist, slightly acidic soil in full sun; keep consistently moist, feed heavily with high-potassium fertilizer, and mulch the root zone heavily in winter in zones 8–9. Musa paradisiaca (plantain banana) is the starchy, cooking banana — botanically a giant herbaceous perennial growing from a massive underground corm, producing a 10–20 foot pseudostem (not a true trunk, but a compressed stem made of overlapping leaf sheaths) topped with enormous, paddle-shaped leaves up to 9 feet long; fruiting occurs 10–18 months after planting when the plant produces a pendant inflorescence that develops into a bunch containing 5–12 hands of 10–20 plantain fruits each. Unlike dessert bananas, plantains are harvested and consumed at all ripeness stages: green plantains are starchy and firm, used for tostones, chips, and savory dishes; yellow plantains are semi-sweet; and fully ripe, black-skinned plantains are sweet enough for desserts. In zone 9, established corms reliably resprout each spring after winter dieback and fruit every 12–18 months from established stools. Plantains are a dietary staple across the Caribbean, West Africa, and Latin America.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Banana and Plantain
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical Fruits
Caribbean Agricultural Research and Development Institute

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in deep, well-drained, fertile, moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; wax apple requires tropical to warm subtropical conditions (zones 10–12) to fruit reliably, though established trees may survive light frosts in zone 9 with damage — protect with frost cloth below 28°F.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; wax apple thrives in tropical lowland conditions with high humidity and warm temperatures year-round. Syzygium samarangense (wax apple or Java apple) is a tropical fruit tree native to the Malay Peninsula and Andaman Islands, widely cultivated throughout Southeast Asia for its distinctive, pear-shaped to bell-shaped fruit with thin, waxy, translucent red to deep-red (or white, green, or pink depending on variety) skin, crispy, spongy, mildly sweet flesh, and a hollow, air-filled core with a rose-water scent — the fresh eating experience is unlike any other fruit, refreshingly crisp and very mildly sweet. The tree is evergreen with large, oval, glossy leaves and produces clusters of showy white to pale-pink, brush-like flowers (typical of the Myrtaceae family) followed by fruits in clusters. In the United States, wax apple cultivation is primarily limited to southern Florida and Hawaii; in zone 9, container culture or greenhouse growing is required. Named varieties include 'Jambu Air' (very dark red, sweet) and 'Pearl' (white-green, milder flavor).
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Fruits
Purdue University — Wax Apple (Fruits of Warm Climates)

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, fertile, moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; ackee requires warm tropical conditions in zones 10–12 to fruit reliably; in zone 9, it may survive in protected locations but rarely fruits without summer heat accumulation.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; avoid planting in frost-prone sites. Blighia sapida (ackee) is a West African tree that became the national fruit of Jamaica, where the arils (fleshy seed coverings) of the opened, ripe fruit are the key ingredient in Jamaica's national dish, ackee and saltfish. The three large, black seeds of each red ackee pod are surrounded by creamy-yellow, buttery, scrambled-egg-textured arils that taste mildly savory and are typically sautéed with salt cod, onions, and peppers. IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE: Unripe ackee (pods not fully opened naturally) contains hypoglycin A, a toxin that can cause Jamaican Vomiting Sickness — consume only the fully ripe arils after the pod has opened completely on the tree and seeds have developed fully. The ripe arils are nutritious, high in protein, fat, and niacin. The tree is an attractive, medium-sized evergreen with pinnate leaves and small white flowers. Fresh ackee is available in Jamaican and Caribbean communities in South Florida; canned ackee (already safely processed) is widely available.
sources: Purdue University — Ackee (Fruits of Warm Climates)
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Fruits
Jamaican Agricultural Research and Development Institute

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees or root cuttings in spring in rich, deep, well-drained, moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; breadnut thrives in warm tropical and subtropical conditions and is best suited to zones 10–12 in the continental US, though protected specimens may survive in zone 9b.

directions: Plant in rich, deep, well-drained, moist, fertile soil in full sun in frost-free tropical conditions; in south Florida, protect young trees from the rare hard freeze. Artocarpus camansi (breadnut) is the seeded ancestor of the seedless breadfruit (A. altilis) and produces large, spiny, oblong to round fruits 8–12 inches long containing 20–50 large, protein-rich seeds embedded in creamy, starchy, edible flesh; both the seeds and flesh are eaten. The seeds (when boiled, roasted, or ground into flour) are the primary food value of breadnut, tasting somewhat like chestnuts or macadamia nuts — they are a traditional protein-rich staple in the Pacific Islands and Philippines (where the tree is called 'kulo'). The flesh (ripe) is sweet and may be eaten similarly to breadfruit. The large, deeply lobed, tropical leaves and rapid growth rate make breadnut an impressive specimen tree in tropical gardens. Breadnut is native to New Guinea and the Philippines and widely naturalized in tropical America; it is closely related to jackfruit, durian, and the edible fig within the family Moraceae.
sources: Purdue University — Breadfruit and Breadnut (Fruits of Warm Climates)
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Artocarpus

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in full sun in well-drained to poorly drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Sesbania grandiflora grows with remarkable speed (4–6 feet per month in warm conditions) and begins flowering within 3–6 months of planting — it is treated as a warm-season annual in zones 8–9 or a short-lived perennial in zone 10–11.

directions: Sow seeds (scarify or nick seed coat to improve germination) directly in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun after last frost; plants grow very rapidly and begin producing edible flowers within 3–6 months. Sesbania grandiflora (vegetable hummingbird or agati) is a fast-growing tropical tree or large shrub grown throughout South and Southeast Asia as one of the most productive edible-flowering plants — producing spectacular, orchid-like flowers 2–4 inches long in white (the typical form) or red, that are harvested and cooked as a vegetable throughout Southeast Asian and Indian cuisines. The flowers are slightly bitter, nutritious (high in calcium, iron, and vitamin C), and used stir-fried, in curries, in salads, and in soups; they are a common street food ingredient in Thailand, India, and the Philippines. All parts of the plant are edible: young leaves are used like spinach, young seed pods like string beans, and the flowers are the most prized part. Sesbania fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules, improving soil fertility. The tree is native to tropical Asia and grows with such speed that it can produce harvestable crops within its first growing season as an annual in temperate climates.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Vegetables
FAO — Traditional Crops of Southeast Asia
